beats.text: You've practiced building expressions from equal groups pictures. You learned different ways to build them: choosing from options and using tiles. Now let's connect what you discovered.

incorrects.description: Student selected 'The containers (bags/boxes/circles)': focused on what changes rather than what stays the same

steps.text: The containers do change. bags, boxes, circles. But look at the expression below. What does 2 × 5 tell us about the groups?

incorrects.description: Student selected 'The items inside': focused on visual details rather than mathematical structure

steps.text: The items might look different. But count the groups in each picture. How many groups? How many in each group? That's what matters.

incorrects.description: Student selected 'The colors or shapes': focused on surface features rather than mathematical structure

steps.text: Colors and shapes can change, but the multiplication stays the same. Look at 2 × 5. Count the groups and what's in each group across all three pictures.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System highlights 2 groups with 5 items in bags.
System highlights 2 groups with 5 items in boxes.
System highlights 2 groups with 5 items in circles.

steps.text: Let me show you. In the bags: 2 groups, 5 in each. In the boxes: 2 groups, 5 in each. In the circles: 2 groups, 5 in each. The containers change, but the structure stays the same. 2 groups with 5 in each. That's what 2 × 5 means.

incorrects.description: Student selected scenario D: park benches with varying numbers of people (not equal groups)

params.description: Scenario D (park benches) highlights to show the unequal groups.

steps.text: Look at those benches. Some have 2 people, some have 3, some have 1. Equal groups need the same number in each. Which scenarios have the same number?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models correct answers.

params.description: Scenarios A, B, and C all highlight to show they are valid equal groups.

steps.text: Let me show you. The egg carton has 2 rows of 6 eggs. The parking lot has 3 rows of 4 cars. The bookshelf has 5 shelves with 3 books each. All three show equal groups because each group has the same number. D doesn't work because the benches have different numbers of people.

beats.text: You built expressions from equal groups pictures. You learned that 4 times 3 means 4 groups with 3 in each. You know what the numbers represent: groups and items.
Next time, you'll find out what 4 times 3 equals. You'll learn to figure out the total. You're ready because you already know what the expression means.
